title: Works on My Machine
intro: It's Friday afternoon. You deploy a feature that passed all local testing. Five minutes later, the app crashes in production -- just like a recipe that works in your kitchen but fails in a restaurant with a different oven. Your console shows an error you've never seen before. Time to debug the most famous phrase in software: 'But it works on my machine!'

teach.title: The Kitchen Analogy
teach.body: Imagine a recipe that works perfectly in your kitchen but fails at a restaurant. Why? Different oven temperature, different altitude, different brand of flour. Your kitchen (development) and the restaurant (production) look similar but have subtle differences. The same happens with code -- your laptop and the server are different **environments** with different settings.

teach.title: The Moving Day Surprise
teach.body: You packed your lamp and it worked great at your old apartment. But at the new place, the outlet voltage is different and the lamp blows out. Your laptop and the production server are like two different apartments -- same lamp (code), different electrical systems (operating system, installed software, configuration). What worked in one place can literally break in the other.

teach.title: The Top 5 'Works on My Machine' Bugs
teach.body:
These are the most common reasons code works locally but fails in production:

1. **Missing environment variables** -- You set them locally but forgot to add them on the server
2. **Case-sensitive file paths** -- Mac/Windows ignore case, Linux doesn't
3. **Different Node.js versions** -- Your laptop has v20, the server has v18
4. **Missing dependencies** -- A package is installed globally on your machine but not in package.json
5. **Hardcoded localhost URLs** -- localhost doesn't exist on the server

teach.title: A Sneaky Bug
teach.annotation: Each of these looks correct on your machine but will crash on the server for different reasons.

teach.body: Knowing how to squash 'works on my machine' bugs means your vibecoded apps actually work for real users -- and real users are the ones who pay you.
'Works on my machine' bugs are caused by differences between your local environment and the server. Always test your build locally, check env vars, and match Node versions.

start.narrative: You deployed a new feature at 4pm on Friday. The deployment succeeded, but users are reporting a blank white page. Your local version works perfectly. The production error log shows: 'TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading "NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL")'. Where do you start?

choices.text: Check if NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL is set in the hosting platform's environment variables
Roll back to the previous version immediately, investigate Monday
Add a try-catch around the code that uses that variable

check-env.narrative: You open the Vercel dashboard and look at the environment variables. NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL is missing! You had it in your local .env file but never added it to Vercel. You add it and redeploy. But the site still shows a blank page. The logs now show: 'Error: connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:5432'. What's happening?

choices.text: DATABASE_URL is pointing to localhost -- it needs the production database URL
The database server is down -- contact the hosting provider
Try restarting the deployment

rollback-first.narrative: Rolling back gets the old version live again -- good instinct for protecting users. But you still need to fix the issue. Looking at the error message, it's about a missing environment variable. This is a configuration problem, not a code problem.

choices.text: Check the environment variables on the hosting platform

band-aid.narrative: A try-catch would silence the error but the feature would still be broken -- it needs that API URL to function. The root cause is that the variable doesn't exist in production. You need to set it up properly.

choices.text: Check the environment variables on the hosting platform

fix-db.narrative: DATABASE_URL was the dev connection string. You update it to the production URL and redeploy. The app boots! But now an internal error fires when uploading images: 'ReferenceError: structuredClone is not defined'. That's a Node API. What's likely going on?

choices.text: The production server is on Node 16, but my code uses APIs that need Node 18+
I forgot to import a polyfill for structuredClone
Production is fine -- the error must be from a stale browser cache

match-node.narrative: You check: your laptop runs Node 20, the platform defaults to Node 16. That's why structuredClone is missing. How do you make sure every developer and every server uses the same version?

choices.text: Set 'engines.node' in package.json AND commit a '.nvmrc' (or 'volta' / 'mise.toml') so local installs match
Just bump Node on the platform dashboard and tell teammates to upgrade Node manually
Avoid the new APIs and stick to Node 16 syntax forever

reproduce-prod.narrative: Versions are now pinned. Before you celebrate, you want to be able to reproduce production-style problems on your laptop instead of finding out from users. What's the most reliable way to do that?

choices.text: Add a 'pnpm start:prod' script that runs 'npm run build && NODE_ENV=production node server.js' against a copy of your prod env vars
Spin up a Docker container that mirrors the production base image and run the build inside it
Just keep using 'npm run dev' and trust that prod will behave the same

prevent-future.narrative: You can now reproduce prod locally. Final move: how do you stop the next 'I forgot to set DATABASE_URL on the platform' from ever shipping?

choices.text: Add a startup health check that lists every required env var; fail loudly at boot if any is missing AND require deploys to pass that check before promotion
Create a .env.example file and update the deploy checklist on Notion
Send a Slack message to the team explaining what happened today

end-good.narrative: You found two missing env vars, fixed the localhost DATABASE_URL, pinned Node, set up a way to run a production build locally, and added a boot-time env-var health check that refuses to start with missing variables. The next missing variable will fail in CI, not in production at 4pm Friday.
end-good.feedback: Senior-level operational instinct. The big move is the boot-time check that fails loud when config is wrong -- it converts a silent runtime error into an obvious deploy failure. Combined with version pinning and a way to reproduce prod locally, you've eliminated the three biggest 'works on my machine' classes.

end-okay.narrative: You shipped a clean fix and documented the variables. Documentation helps onboarding, but it doesn't enforce anything -- the next deploy can still ship missing env vars without anyone noticing until users do.
end-okay.feedback: Solid recovery. .env.example is industry standard, but it's still a manual safeguard. A startup health check that refuses to boot with missing config is the active version of the same idea -- and it never gets skipped, even on a Friday at 4pm.

end-weak.narrative: Today's bug is fixed, but nothing structural changed. The next person to deploy a feature that needs a new env var will get the same blank-page incident, and you'll spend another Friday on the same kind of debugging.
end-weak.feedback: You handled the immediate fire but didn't reduce the surface of future fires. Tribal knowledge in Slack messages does not scale. A startup health check or a CI step that validates env vars is the cheap, durable fix.
